# Principle 8: Separate Generate & Evaluate


> **Separate the generating agent from the evaluating agent. The same agent evaluating its own output will be biased.**

When Claude evaluates something it just produced, it applies implicit leniency — "I just built this, so it's probably good enough." This isn't a model defect; it's a structural problem.

The fix is a completely separate Evaluator Agent. Anthropic's internal experiments showed meaningful quality differences between a single agent doing both generation and evaluation versus a dedicated Evaluator Agent doing the evaluation.

The `adversarial-review` Skill is the canonical implementation of this principle: a subagent with "fresh eyes" is summoned separately to critique, revisions are made, and the loop repeats until findings are trivial. Generator and evaluator are structurally separated.

**Bad**: "Write this code, then review it yourself and mark it complete if you find no issues" — one agent handles both generation and evaluation.

**Good**:
```
# Generator Agent → produces output
# Evaluator Agent (separate session, fresh context) → produces critique list
# Generator Agent → revises based on critique list
# Repeat until findings are trivial
```
